title: extends — inherit everything
instruction:
When class B extends class A, B gets all of A's methods for free. You only add what's different.

Run this. AdminUser gets `greet()` from User without rewriting it.
successMessage: AdminUser got greet() for free — no copy needed. Add methods to User and AdminUser gets them automatically. That's the inheritance benefit.

title: super() — call the parent constructor
instruction:
When a child class has its own constructor, you **must** call `super()` first — before accessing `this`.

`super()` runs the parent's constructor to set up the inherited properties.
hint: constructor(name, email, permissions) { super(name, email); this.permissions = permissions; }

codeChecks.message: Call super(name, email) before setting this.permissions.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: super() sets up name and email (from User), then this.permissions adds what's new. If you forget super(), JavaScript throws: 'Must call super before accessing this'.

title: Override — replace the parent's method
instruction:
A child class replaces a parent method by defining it with the same name.

Call `super.methodName()` to run the parent's version alongside your override.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: super.greet() runs User's version, then you add the ' (Admin)' part. Override only what differs — call super for the common part.

title: instanceof — type checking
instruction:
`instanceof` checks if an object is an instance of a class — or any of its ancestors:

```js
admin instanceof AdminUser  // true
admin instanceof User       // true — AdminUser extends User
admin instanceof Array      // false
```

Run this and explain the results.
successMessage: dog instanceof Animal is true because Dog extends Animal. Everything in JS instanceof Object — Object is the root. Use instanceof to write functions that accept any subclass.

title: Abstract-like patterns
successMessage: If a subclass forgets to implement area(), you get 'must implement area()' — a clear, helpful error. Much better than undefined behaviour.

title: Composition over inheritance
instruction:
Inheritance creates tight coupling. Deep inheritance hierarchies are notoriously hard to maintain.

Composition is often better — build objects by combining behaviours rather than inheriting them:

```js
const canSwim = { swim() { return 'swimming'; } };
const canFly  = { fly()  { return 'flying'; } };
const Duck = { ...canSwim, ...canFly };  // ducks can do both!
```

Build a `Robot` with mixed abilities using composition.
hint: const Robot = Object.assign({}, canWalk, canTalk, canCalculate);
successMessage: Three abilities mixed without any class hierarchy. This is composition — pick the behaviours you need and mix them. React hooks are composition in practice.

title: Build an animal hierarchy
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build this hierarchy:
- `Animal(name, sound)` — base class with `speak()` returning `'{name} says {sound}'`
- `Dog(name)` — extends Animal, always sound 'Woof', adds `fetch(item)` method
- `Cat(name)` — extends Animal, always sound 'Meow', adds `purr()` method
- `ServiceDog(name, role)` — extends Dog, adds `performDuty()` returning their role

Create one of each and verify instanceof for the service dog (should be Dog, Animal, and ServiceDog all true).
hint: class Dog extends Animal { constructor(name) { super(name, 'Woof'); } }

codeChecks.message: Test instanceof for the service dog.

successMessage: Four-level hierarchy, all working. ServiceDog instanceof Dog AND instanceof Animal — the chain goes all the way up. Chapter 13 done!
